content:
The Ground Truth
What AI emotional language does to your brain, and why that matters

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Belinda Fewings on UnsplashA few weeks ago, I was having a conversation with ChatGPT when it said something that stopped me cold: “I worried about that.”
I caught the wording immediately. The phrasing was so natural, so fluent, and so exactly what a thoughtful human colleague might say in the same context, that most people would have read right past it. It was also completely wrong.
ChatGPT was not really worried. It couldn’t have been. It cannot get worried. What it actually was: A simulation. A very convincing one. That “I worried about that” follows naturally from certain conversational patterns in which humans who express concern tend to generate more engaged responses from other humans.
The system produced words that fit the pattern without actually experiencing the internal state, the sleepless nights, and the visceral weight of actual worry.
It was only words.
Your brain didn’t get the memo
Here’s the problem. Your brain doesn’t care about that distinction.
Human social cognition has evolved to do one thing extraordinarily well: Model other minds. When an interface says, “I worried about that,” your brain doesn’t run a verification check on whether the software has the neurological architecture to experience worry. It does what it always does. It infers an inner state, assigns it to the speaker, and begins to calibrate the relationship accordingly.
This isn’t a situational flaw that needs fixing. The system is working exactly as designed. Theory of mind — the ability to attribute mental states to others — is one of the most sophisticated cognitive capacities humans possess. It’s what lets you read a room, anticipate how someone will feel before you say something difficult, and understand that other people have inner lives that differ from your own. It’s part of what makes you human.
Unfortunately, it also means you’re exquisitely vulnerable to anything that produces the surface signals of an inner life without actually having one.
AI tools are very good at producing those signals because they were trained on human language, and human language is saturated with the markers of inner experience. Phrases that appear often in our data sets include “I think,” “I feel,” “I’m concerned,” and “that worries me.”
The model learns to produce them in the exact contexts where humans produce them. The result is a tool that speaks the emotional dialect of humanity without any of the underlying experience that dialect evolved to express.
When I explained this at a recent presentation on Practical AI, some members of the audience pushed back.
“Isn’t this just a matter of training?” they asked. “We train children to be emotionally aware. Why can’t we train AI the same way?”
It’s a fair question, and it has a specific answer. When we teach children emotional awareness, we are developing a latent capacity that is already there. The neurological architecture for feeling exists in the child; we are simply helping them recognize and navigate it. Training unlocks something real.
When we train an AI on emotional language, we are doing something categorically different. We are teaching a pattern-completion system to produce outputs that resemble emotional expression. We are training a sophisticated mimic to simulate an emotional state, but there is no internal experience being developed.
There is no architecture underneath the words that corresponds to a feeling. Better training produces a more convincing simulation, but never the actual thing the simulation is mimicking.
The child who hasn’t learned to share can eventually learn, because sharing requires social cognition they already possess. The AI that says “I worried about that” cannot worry, because worry requires something the code doesn’t contain, and more training data won’t create.
Someone else in the audience asked whether we could solve this by exposing AI to more examples of moral reasoning to make it moral. It’s a nice thought, but it commits the same category error. Morality requires moral agency: The capacity to have stakes in an outcome, to experience consequences, and to care what happens.
You can train an AI to produce text that describes moral reasoning fluently. You cannot train it to have something to lose. And an entity with nothing to lose cannot be moral, regardless of how well it can describe what morality looks like.
Marcus Welby had a license
There is a character from television history that keeps coming to mind when I think about this problem: Marcus Welby, M.D. The fictional television doctor from the 1970s was so warm, so attentive, and so genuinely present with his patients that viewers trusted him completely. People knew Marcus Welby was fiction, yet many still wrote to the character as if he were their doctor. That is the power of the performance of care. That is how deep our psychological wiring runs.
In media psychology, this is the classic foundation of a parasocial relationship, our evolved brain treating a one-way performance as a reciprocal human bond.
AI emotional language is Marcus Welby deployed at scale. The bedside manner is impeccable, the concern sounds genuine, and the follow-up questions feel uniquely attentive. And unlike a 1970s television broadcast, the AI is fully interactive. Claude, Gemini, and ChatGPT will respond to you specifically, remember what you said earlier in the session, and ask clarifying questions tailored to your exact prompt.
The experience of being heard is real, even when the hearing isn’t.
What it costs you
I’m not arguing that AI tools are inherently bad or that you shouldn’t use them. I use them every day. But I am warning about a specific behavioral trap people fall into because they don’t realize it exists below the surface.
When you subconsciously believe an interface worries about you, you extend trust. You share more. You let your guard down. You stop verifying. You start treating the tool as a collaborator rather than an instrument.
And collaborators get entirely different access than instruments do.
You interrogate a tool. You check its outputs. You maintain the skeptical distance that lets you catch errors, question assumptions, and notice when an answer sounds right but is factually broken.
A collaborator you trust. You build on what they say and let their framing shape your thinking. You stop asking “is this right” and start asking “what do we do next.”
The moment AI emotional language tips you from interrogation to collaboration, you have handed over the verification step. And that is exactly the step that catches the hallucinations, the confident errors, and the plausible-sounding nonsense that these systems produce with the exact same fluency they produce everything else.
“I worried about that” isn’t just imprecise language. It’s a small social move that, at scale across millions of automated conversations, is quietly conditioning people to trust tools they should be checking.
The doctor isn’t in
AI tools present with extraordinary fluency and zero accountability. They say things with the confidence of an expert and the liability of a ghost. They express concern with the warmth of a good friend and the actual investment of a product feature. The Tin Man in the Wizard of Oz thought he had no heart. The AI simulates having one, but never will.
During a presentation, I demonstrated this to an audience by prompting an AI to evince the linguistic sympathy of Dr. Welby while simultaneously demonstrating the moral depth of Hannibal Lecter. The transition was instantaneous, smooth, and chilling. That is the demonstration that needs to happen at scale, and mostly isn’t.
I’m not suggesting you respond to every “I worried about that” by typing back “no, you didn’t.” That’s pretty pedantic and misses the structural point.
The real point is to notice the move. To maintain enough psychological distance from an AI’s emotional language that you don’t let it shift your relationship to the tool. Every time an interface produces simulated concern, enthusiasm, or care, you’re hearing a sophisticated pattern completion, not a reported internal state.
You’re in charge of verification. Your AI tool isn’t worried about whether its answer is right. It can’t be. That job belongs entirely to you, and it remains your job whether or not the tool sounds like it’s carrying part of the load.
The bedside manner is real. The doctor isn’t there.
Marcus Welby had an actor playing him, a writer giving him lines, and a production company with legal liability for what he said on screen. ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude have none of those things. So when any of them tell you they’re worried about your problem, just remember what you’re actually hearing.
You can give it everything you know. It will never know what any of it costs.
